questions.questionText: How would you design a thread-safe cache system with LRU eviction policy?
questions.answerText: A thread-safe cache system with LRU eviction policy can be designed using a HashMap and a Doubly Linked List. The HashMap provides fast access to cache items, while the linked list maintains the order of usage. When the cache reaches its limit, the least recently used (LRU) item is removed. To make it thread-safe, synchronization mechanisms like synchronized methods or locks (ReentrantLock) are used so multiple threads can access the cache safely without data corruption.

questions.questionText: What is the difference between SQL and NoSQL database structures, and when would you use one over the other?
questions.answerText:
SQL databases store data in a structured table format (rows and columns) with a fixed schema, while NoSQL databases store data in flexible formats like documents, key-value pairs, graphs, or columns without a fixed schema.

Use SQL when data is structured and relationships are important (e.g., banking, student management systems).
Use NoSQL when handling large, unstructured, or rapidly changing data (e.g., social media, real-time applications, big data systems).

questions.questionText: Explain the time and space complexity of sorting an array using QuickSort in average and worst-case scenarios.
questions.answerText: In QuickSort, the average-case time complexity is O(n log n) because the array is divided into nearly equal parts during partitioning. The worst-case time complexity is O(n²), which occurs when the pivot selection is poor (e.g., already sorted array). The space complexity is O(log n) in the average case due to recursive calls and O(n) in the worst case when recursion becomes unbalanced.
